food_subgroup: Cooked crustaceans — stir-fried or sautéed prawns with spice paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pepper prawn; closest parent is shrimp food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A02GJ covers cooked crustaceans as the nearest parent; no specific pepper prawn entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for pepper prawn or equivalent prepared prawn dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pepper prawn in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising 5–8 whole prawns cooked in black pepper sauce, served as a main dish or shared side at a hawker centre or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked Prawns / Shrimp — nutrient profile adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat) and HPB Singapore Foodo reference values for stir-fried prawn dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base prawn nutrient values taken from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked shrimp, moist heat). Values adjusted upward for fat and sodium to account for stir-frying in oil with black pepper, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo guidance for stir-fried seafood dishes. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate for cooked prawns/shrimp (protein-dominant, negligible starch food)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pepper prawn. Prawns are a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate food with negligible starch content. GI is estimated at 5 by analogy with other cooked crustacean and high-protein seafood dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 4.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 0.42, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1040 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce sodium intake, request reduced-sauce preparation, and avoid adding extra soy sauce, as a single 200 g serving may contribute approximately 45% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g) and negligible glycemic index (~5), resulting in minimal postprandial glucose impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pepper prawn is a suitable protein-rich, low-glycemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, sodium from the sauce should be monitored, and the dish is best paired with a low-GI carbohydrate source such as brown rice to maintain overall glycemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content (~152 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium load; partially offset by low saturated fat and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume pepper prawn in moderate portions (100–150 g) as prawns provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, but should be mindful of the cholesterol content and high sodium from the sauce preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pepper Prawn is a popular Singaporean seafood dish consisting of whole prawns stir-fried in a robust sauce made from freshly cracked black pepper, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, typically cooked in a wok over high heat. The dish delivers a bold, pungent, and slightly spicy flavour profile with a savoury umami depth from the fermented sauces. The prawns retain a firm, succulent texture when properly wok-fried. Nutritionally, pepper prawn is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 17.8 g of protein per 100 g, making it suitable for individuals seeking lean protein sources. The fat content is moderate at 6.5 g per 100 g, primarily from cooking oil, with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio. The glycemic index is very low (estimated ~5), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce and oyster sauce in the preparation. Pepper prawn is commonly served as a main course or shared dish in Singapore hawker centres, seafood restaurants, and zi char stalls. It is culturally associated with celebratory meals and is a staple of Singapore's seafood cuisine alongside chilli crab and salted egg prawns. The dish is naturally gluten-containing due to oyster sauce and is not suitable for those with shellfish or crustacean allergies.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for pepper prawn was located. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked shrimp data with adjustments for stir-frying with oil and high-sodium sauces, consistent with HPB guidance for stir-fried seafood. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct primary-source match for this specific prepared dish.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked shrimp and is not routinely measured in Singapore HPB Foodo entries for stir-fried seafood. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked shrimp with no hydrogenated oils in the preparation. Sodium value (~520 mg per 100 g) reflects adaptation for soy sauce and oyster sauce addition during stir-frying; the base cooked shrimp value from USDA is approximately 224 mg per 100 g. GI is estimated at 5 by analogy with cooked crustacean and high-protein seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for pepper prawn exists in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 4.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 0.42, rounded to 0.4. Halal status is assumed based on prawn being permissible under most Sunni Islamic jurisprudence and the absence of pork-derived ingredients; oyster sauce brand may vary and consumers should verify halal certification. The dish is served by Maharani Table - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 18.90.
